metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the env var name for overlap_drain opt-out to a single canonical value across c1de's What section and AC4 (either REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN or REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN, consistently).
findings.finding: c1de (S4) uses two different environment variable names for the same opt-out flag within the same child: the What section specifies `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN` while AC4 specifies `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN`. This is an internal inconsistency that will produce a broken opt-out if the implementer follows one source and ignores the other.
findings.location: child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135, What section ('env REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_DRAIN') vs Acceptance Criteria AC4 ('env REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN')
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one name (suggest `REBAR_OVERLAP_DRAIN` for consistency with `REBAR_OVERLAP_*` namespace) and use it in both the What section and AC4 of c1de.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate a single child (or explicit merge order) as the canonical owner of the EVENT_TYPES and _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES edits, or add cross-references in both 2d0f and e1f4 acknowledging the shared-line edits and stating required merge order.
findings.finding: Both 2d0f (S2) and e1f4 (S3) independently modify the same two lines in two shared files — `EVENT_TYPES` list in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (~line 38) and `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` list in `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py` (line 44) — with no designated single owner for either file. Neither child acknowledges the other's concurrent edit to the same lines.
findings.location: child 2d0f-19f2-dd15-4336, Scope ('src/rebar/_store/event_append.py: add TICKET_DIGEST to EVENT_TYPES', 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py: add TICKET_DIGEST to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES') and child e1f4-d518-3240-472e, Scope ('src/rebar/_store/event_append.py EVENT_TYPES: add the three types', 'src/rebar/reducer/_version.py: add them to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 2d0f's scope: 'Note: e1f4 also adds to EVENT_TYPES and _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES; 2d0f should land first (as e1f4 depends on 2d0f), so 2d0f establishes the TICKET_DIGEST entry and e1f4 adds its three types on top in a subsequent commit.' Mirror this note in e1f4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] c1de must specify a single canonical invocation hook point for the opportunistic drain (e.g., 'the post-write path of claim, mirroring push.py line 108') and remove the 'or' ambiguity.
findings.finding: c1de (S4) specifies the opportunistic drain hook attachment point as 'the same post-write path as push (or to review_plan/claim)' — the 'or' leaves the exact hook location unowned and ambiguous. No sibling assigns or resolves this choice, meaning the drain could be double-hooked (both review_plan AND claim) or missed entirely.
findings.location: child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135, Scope section — opportunistic hook attachment point
findings.suggested_fix: Pin the hook to one site: 'attached to the same post-command path as push.py (src/rebar/_store/push.py:108), triggered on claim' — or explicitly to review_plan if that is the intent, and note in 0f70 that the drain hook is wired there.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe AC1 as an observable end-state (e.g., 'Running the overlap detector against the labeled fixture corpus returns a suggestion containing the known duplicate pair, logged as advisory output during the plan-review window') rather than a delivery milestone.
findings.finding: AC1 is delivery-framed ('delivered across S1–S7') rather than stating a specific observable outcome; an executor cannot determine what passing looks like without inspecting the child stories.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'Detects semantic overlap/duplication... (delivered across S1–S7)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(delivered across S1–S7)' with a deterministic check: e.g., 'Verified by running `rebar overlap-check <ticket-id>` against the labeled fixture from S6 and confirming at least one known duplicate pair appears in advisory output, with no auto-mutation of links.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that verifies the labeled-pair fixture (S6) exists and that a related-but-distinct pair is NOT surfaced by the detector (i.e., precision gate passes).
findings.finding: The Success Criterion for precision/false-flagging ('Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work: precision is measured on a labeled fixture of ticket pairs (see S6), and a related-but-distinct pair must not be surfaced') describes a concrete deliverable — a labeled fixture and a precision gate — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies it. The four ACs cover detection, suggestion surfacing, advisory placement, and the ADR, but none covers the labeled-fixture precision measurement or the must-not-surface rule for related-but-distinct pairs.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3 (precision/false-flagging) vs. Acceptance Criteria section (all four bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A labeled fixture of ticket pairs (owned by S6) exists and the detector does not surface a known related-but-distinct pair as a duplicate/supersede/depends_on candidate (precision gate passes on the fixture).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 0f70 ADR AC must enumerate all four required architectural topics (Cupid-digest-without-embeddings, event-store-as-enrichment-queue, digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar, overlap-at-review-time-not-as-5fd2-criterion) as explicit subsection or checklist requirements, not just file existence.
findings.finding: 0f70's ADR acceptance criterion ('the architecture ADR docs/adr/NNNN-cross-ticket-overlap.md is written') does not verify that ALL four ADR topics named in parent SC5 are captured: (1) Cupid-digest-without-embeddings, (2) event-store-as-enrichment-queue, (3) digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar, (4) overlap-at-review-time-not-as-a-5fd2-criterion. The AC is pass/fail on existence only, not on content — a structurally incomplete coverage of SC5 by the three-part standard (not measurable in the same terms).
findings.location: child 0f70-6d61-2b78-4c34, Acceptance Criteria — ADR bullet; parent SC5
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the ADR AC to: 'The architecture ADR docs/adr/NNNN-cross-ticket-overlap.md is written and contains sections covering: (1) Cupid-digest-without-embeddings rationale, (2) event-store-as-enrichment-queue rationale, (3) digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar rationale, (4) overlap-at-review-time-not-as-a-5fd2-criterion rationale.'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (suggest ee3d as the leaf/foundation, or a new minimal S0 task) as the canonical owner of the overlap config section in VerifyConfig, with a complete field inventory; all other children reference that section rather than independently adding fields.
findings.finding: Six children (ee3d, 2d0f, 5a8f, 9022, c1de, e1f4, 0f70) all independently add `overlap_*` config fields to `VerifyConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with no single child designated as the canonical schema owner for the overlap config section. The field names are described inconsistently (some use `overlap_` prefix, some do not: e1f4 uses bare `SOAK`/`LEASE_TTL` in prose but implies `overlap_*` namespace in scope).
findings.location: children ee3d-fc7b-e280-4cbb (overlap_propositions_min/max), 5a8f-d6a0-ca32-4f51 (overlap field weights/K/floor/max_doc_freq), 9022-d3e3-e97f-47e3 (overlap_conf_threshold, overlap_surface_cap), c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135 (overlap_drain), e1f4-d518-3240-472e (SOAK, LEASE_TTL), 0f70-6d61-2b78-4c34 (overlap_enabled) — all in src/rebar/_config_schema.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit 'overlap config section' subsection to ee3d (the foundational child with no dependencies) that enumerates all overlap_* fields across the epic with their types, defaults, and env var names. All other children reference 'add to the overlap config section defined in S1' rather than independently editing _config_schema.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the parameter name and type of pending_enrichment between c1de (uses 'now') and e1f4 (defines 'now_ns' as nanosecond integer); e1f4's AC should also use 'now_ns' consistently.
findings.finding: c1de (S4) calls the queue-read function `pending_enrichment(now)` while e1f4 (S3) defines it as `pending_enrichment(now_ns)`. The parameter name difference implies a different type: `now` suggests a datetime or float seconds, while `now_ns` explicitly requires a nanosecond integer. If the caller (c1de) passes a datetime or float to the function expecting ns-integer, the soak boundary comparison silently misfires.
findings.location: child c1de-d6a0-6cef-4135, What section ('from pending_enrichment(now)') vs child e1f4-d518-3240-472e, What/Scope ('pending_enrichment(now_ns)')
findings.suggested_fix: Standardize on `pending_enrichment(now_ns: int)` everywhere, and update c1de's What section to use `pending_enrichment(now_ns=time.time_ns())`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the initial corpus seed (digest generation for all pre-existing tickets) is batched/throttled (e.g., N tickets per minute with a configurable cap), not issued as a single bulk operation, and document the expected seed duration/cost at current store size.
findings.finding: The plan never explicitly specifies that the initial corpus backfill (generating Cupid digests for ALL pre-existing tickets) is batched or throttled rather than issued as a single bulk operation at deploy time.
findings.location: Design section — 'each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest'; 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'
findings.suggested_fix: In S1 or a dedicated seed-migration story, add an explicit seed script that pages through existing tickets in configurable batches (e.g., 50 at a time with a 1-second inter-batch delay), enqueuing digest-generation tasks rather than calling the LLM directly in a loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider pulling precision measurement (labeled fixture + judge evaluation, currently S6) earlier in the child sequence — ideally after the LLM judge is first wired (possibly as S2 or S3) — so judge quality gates further infrastructure investment rather than validating it last.
findings.finding: Precision/quality validation (S6, labeled fixture + judge evaluation) appears sequenced near the END of seven children, meaning up to five child stories could be built and integrated before the riskiest assumption — that the LLM pairwise judge achieves acceptable precision — is validated. This is the opposite of evidence-gated MVP: the highest-uncertainty component (judge quality) is tested last, not first.
findings.location: Design section / Success Criteria SC3 / child S6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 0f70 dependency list must include S2 (2d0f, digest store) as a prerequisite alongside S1/S5/S6.
findings.finding: 0f70 (S7) lists its dependencies as 'S1 (enrich), S5 (retrieve), S6 (judge)' but omits S2 (2d0f, the digest store). S7 calls retrieve (S5) which reads the corpus of digests from the S2 sidecar store — making S2 an undeclared upstream dependency for S7. If S2 is not yet merged when S7 is implemented, the corpus read will fail silently (empty corpus, no overlap results) with no signal to the implementer.
findings.location: child 0f70-6d61-2b78-4c34, Dependencies section
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'S2 (digest store — corpus of digests that retrieve reads)' to 0f70's Dependencies section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete precision floor and minimum fixture size for the false-flagging criterion (e.g., '≥80% precision on a labeled fixture of ≥10 related-but-distinct pairs'), so S6 has an unambiguous pass/fail threshold.
findings.finding: The false-flagging precision criterion lacks a numeric floor, making it un-measurable: 'a related-but-distinct pair must not be surfaced' is phrased as an absolute for a single example pair, but does not state a minimum precision percentage or minimum fixture size against which the labeled fixture check is evaluated.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Success Criterion: 'Precision ≥ 80% on a labeled fixture of ≥ 10 related-but-distinct ticket pairs (defined in S6); all pairs in the fixture classified as related-but-distinct must not appear in advisory output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the fallback behavior when a ticket's Cupid digest is absent at query time (e.g., 'skip uncached tickets and emit a warning listing how many were skipped'), so executors and reviewers know what to expect in cold-start or lagging-queue scenarios.
findings.finding: The fallback behavior when a Cupid digest is not yet cached (enrichment queue not yet drained) at plan-review time is unstated, leaving an executor unable to determine what the system does in the common cold-start or lagging-queue scenario.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line fallback statement to the Design section: e.g., 'If a ticket's Cupid digest is absent at query time, it is excluded from the candidate set and a warning is emitted (e.g., "N tickets skipped — digests not yet available") so the operator knows coverage is partial.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement (in S7 or a dedicated AC) for contributor-facing usage documentation covering: how the store-wide overlap step is invoked, what its output format looks like, and how to act on the surfaced `rebar link` suggestions — distinct from the architectural ADR.
findings.finding: The plan mandates an ADR capturing the architecture but does not explicitly require any operator/contributor usage documentation — e.g., how the store-wide step is invoked, what its output looks like, and how a reviewer acts on the surfaced `rebar link` suggestions. The ADR scope is scoped to architectural decisions, leaving the operational/usage contract undocumented.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 5 / AC bullet 4 (ADR requirement) and Design section ('surfaces ≤3 advisory link suggestions for human confirmation')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line such as: 'A usage/integration note (inline in the ADR or as a separate section in docs/) documents the output format of the overlap step, how to invoke it, and the expected human response to advisory suggestions — so contributors can act on the output without reading the implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a recall@20 measurement to the S6 fixture (or a dedicated spike) that verifies BM25F over Cupid-digest text surfaces known-overlapping ticket pairs within the top 20 candidates, so the retrieval stage's failure mode is empirically validated before the full pipeline is built.
findings.finding: The BM25F recall@20 stage — the plan's assertion that brute-force BM25F over Cupid-digest text will reliably surface all semantically overlapping tickets within the top ~20 candidates — is unvalidated before full build-out. The S6 fixture measures precision of surfaced results but not recall of the BM25F candidate selection stage; a recall failure would be silent (overlapping tickets never reach the LLM judge) yet undetected by the precision fixture.
findings.location: Design section: 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'; Success Criteria: 'Low false-flagging... precision is measured on a labeled fixture (see S6)'
findings.suggested_fix: In S6 (or as an explicit pre-S1 spike), include a labeled set of ticket pairs that ARE overlapping but use divergent terminology; run BM25F over their digests and measure recall@20. Define a minimum recall threshold (e.g. 90%) that must pass before the BM25F+digest approach is considered validated. If recall is insufficient, the design doc should note the fallback (e.g. expand digest vocabulary, add query expansion, or adjust K).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mark the BM25F candidate cutoff (~20) and advisory suggestions cap (≤3) as named, configurable constants (e.g. in a config file or environment variable) rather than hardcoded literals, so post-S6 tuning does not require code changes.
findings.finding: The BM25F candidate cutoff (~20) and the maximum advisory suggestions cap (≤3) are asserted as fixed values with no indication they are configurable; given S6 will measure precision and the system is expected to be tuned over time, hardcoding these thresholds risks requiring code changes for routine calibration.
findings.location: Design section: 'narrows to ~20 candidates'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Surfaces ≤3 candidate... relations'
findings.suggested_fix: Define `OVERLAP_BM25_CANDIDATE_K` (default 20) and `OVERLAP_MAX_SUGGESTIONS` (default 3) as named configuration values (e.g. in a `.rebar/config` or environment variable), and reference them from the ADR (S7) as tunable defaults derived from S6 measurement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete precision/recall threshold to the 'low false-flagging' Success Criterion (e.g., 'precision ≥ X% on the labeled S6 fixture') so the goal is end-state-observable.
findings.finding: The 'low false-flagging' success criterion lacks a quantitative precision threshold, leaving the goal without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Success Criteria — item 3: 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend SC item 3 to read: 'precision ≥ [N]% on the labeled S6 fixture; no labeled related-but-distinct pair may be surfaced as a duplicate/supersedes candidate.' Choose N based on the research reports already cited in the plan-of-record comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the precision target from an implicit measurement to an explicit, numbered AC line (e.g., 'S6 fixture shows precision ≥ N% on overlap pairs and 0 false positives on related-but-distinct pairs') so value is measurably validated.
findings.finding: The validation mechanism for user value (labeled fixture, S6) lacks a quantitative acceptance target, making the value-validation incomplete.
findings.location: Success Criteria — item 3 and AC (no corresponding AC line for precision target)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retrieval-recall spike or labeled recall fixture (e.g., known-overlapping pairs from the store's existing manually-linked tickets) that measures BM25F recall@20 on real ticket text BEFORE committing to the ~20-candidate cap and BM25F as the sole retrieval stage — so stage-1 failure mode is empirically validated.
findings.finding: The BM25F stage-1 retrieval recall is asserted (narrows to ~20 candidates) but never validated against actual ticket text. BM25F on short, jargon-heavy ticket descriptions is a novel, unverified assumption; a retrieval failure is invisible to the stage-2 LLM judge and the S6 precision fixture — so the core value proposition (detect semantic overlap) can silently fail for vocabulary-mismatched tickets with no measurement gate catching it.
findings.location: Design section: 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'; Success Criteria: 'precision is measured on a labeled fixture (see S6)'
findings.suggested_fix: In S1 or as a pre-S1 spike, build a small labeled fixture of known-overlapping ticket pairs, run BM25F retrieval, and measure recall@k for k=10,20,30. Record the result in the ADR (S7) and let it drive the candidate-cap choice. If recall@20 < an acceptable threshold, consider expanding k or adding a lightweight embedding fallback. This gates further infrastructure investment on the riskiest unvalidated assumption.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete relation enum the pairwise judge is allowed to emit (aligned with `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` in `src/rebar/graph/_links.py`) and state which values are excluded and why — so the generated `rebar link` commands are guaranteed to be valid and semantically correct.
findings.finding: The pairwise judge's output relation enum is structurally incomplete: the plan names only 3 values (`duplicates`, `supersedes`, `depends_on`) but the live `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` frozenset has 6 (`blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`). An executor building the judge's output schema cannot determine whether `relates_to`, `blocks`, and `discovered_from` are forbidden, mapped, or included — meaning the generated `rebar link` commands may produce semantically wrong or outright invalid relation types when executed by a human.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 2 / AC line 2: 'Surfaces ≤3 candidate duplicate/supersede/dependency relations for human confirmation with copy-paste `rebar link` commands'
findings.suggested_fix: Enumerate all six CANONICAL_RELATIONS values and state the judge's output policy for each: e.g., 'duplicates and supersedes for exact/replacement overlap; depends_on for asymmetric dependency; relates_to for partial thematic overlap; blocks and discovered_from are excluded from judge output (too broad / provenance-only).' This closes the structural gap without over-constraining the design.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the Design section explaining why the existing `scan_epics_for_spec` / `spec_alignment` reviewer path is insufficient for this use case (e.g., scale/cost at whole-store scope, lack of per-ticket retrieval focus, no BM25F pre-filter) — so the chosen Cupid+BM25F+judge approach is defensibly superior rather than assumed.
findings.finding: The plan proposes building a new three-component overlap pipeline (Cupid digest → BM25F retrieve → LLM pairwise judge) without ruling out existing codebase mechanisms that already detect store-wide semantic overlap. `scan_epics_for_spec` (src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py) already evaluates open epics for scope overlaps against an arbitrary spec text and could be called with the query ticket's plan as the spec; the `spec_alignment` reviewer (src/rebar/llm/reviewers/spec_alignment.md) explicitly detects 'scope overlaps: epics that duplicate each other's scope'; and the G4 plan-review criterion already fires a scope-overlap mode. None of these are mentioned. The plan provides no positive rationale for why a new digest/retrieve/judge stack is needed over composing existing mechanisms.
findings.location: Design section: 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank: (0) each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest … (1) brute-force BM25F … (2) a bounded LLM pairwise judge'; Context: 'Existing coverage is partial and different in kind'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Why not existing overlap detectors?' subsection, e.g.: 'scan_epics_for_spec works against a small batch of epics (O(N/batch_size) LLM calls, batch_size=5); at store-wide scope (potentially hundreds of tickets) it becomes cost-prohibitive. The BM25F pre-filter reduces the LLM call count to ≤20 pairwise judgments regardless of store size. Additionally, spec_alignment requires a spec document; this feature works against the ticket under review as a first-class subject without a pre-authored spec.' State this in the ADR (S7) and here in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief differentiation statement explaining why `scan_epics_for_spec` / the `spec_alignment` reviewer (already shipped at `src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py`) is insufficient for this use case and what this new pipeline adds beyond it (e.g., ticket-type coverage beyond epics, no spec required, `rebar link` command output, unconditional rather than spec-gated).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a store-wide overlap detector without acknowledging the already-shipped `scan_epics_for_spec` operation (`src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py`) and its `spec_alignment` reviewer, which explicitly detects scope overlaps across all open epics store-wide — the same problem space. The plan's 'Context' frames the residual as 'uncovered' but does not explain why `scan_epics_for_spec` is insufficient (e.g., it is spec-gated rather than unconditional, it covers only epics rather than all ticket types, it does not output `rebar link` commands), leaving it ambiguous whether this is additive or partially repackaging existing functionality.
findings.location: Plan 'Context' section ('The residual — semantic overlap / duplication / hidden cross-ticket dependency across UNRELATED tickets — is uncovered') and 'Design' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Context' or 'Design' section, add one sentence such as: '`scan_epics_for_spec` (src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py) detects spec-relative overlaps across open epics only; this epic adds unconditional, spec-free semantic overlap detection across ALL ticket types, with `rebar link` command output for human confirmation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the BM25F capability assertion before S2 implementation begins: name the specific Python library that provides BM25F field-weighting (or confirm a bespoke implementation is in scope), reconcile with the 'NO new dependency' claim, and if only standard BM25 (not BM25F) is achievable without a new dep, state why standard BM25 suffices for the required recall@20.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'brute-force BM25F' as the Stage-1 retrieval mechanism while simultaneously claiming 'NO new dependency.' BM25F is a field-weighted variant of BM25 that is NOT provided by standard Python BM25 libraries (rank_bm25 provides BM25Okapi/BM25Plus/BM25L but not BM25F with named field weights). No BM25 library of any kind appears in pyproject.toml. This is an unverified capability assertion: either (a) a bespoke BM25F implementation is intended (adding implementation complexity not scoped), or (b) an existing library is used (requiring a new dependency), or (c) a simpler scoring function (standard BM25 or TF-IDF) would suffice. None of these paths is resolved in the plan.
findings.location: Plan 'Design' section: 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'; plan-of-record comment: 'BM25F "More Like This" of the query ticket's digest against the corpus digests (title/problem-statement weighted high)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Design' section, either: (a) name the specific library (e.g., 'rank_bm25, adding it to the [agents] extra') and remove the 'NO new dependency' claim for BM25; or (b) replace 'BM25F' with 'BM25 (title/problem-statement fields weighted via pre-processing: prepend field tokens N times before tokenization)' and confirm this is achievable with a stdlib-only or already-present dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that digest generation is idempotent: the enrichment worker checks for an existing valid digest before calling the LLM and skips re-generation unless a force-refresh flag is set.
findings.finding: The plan does not state that the digest-backfill operation is idempotent/resumable — i.e., that re-running after a partial failure skips already-digested tickets rather than re-processing or double-writing them.
findings.location: Design section — digest caching; Tier 1 enrichment queue
findings.suggested_fix: Record a `digested_at` timestamp alongside each Cupid digest; the worker's first step is: `if digest exists and not force_refresh: skip`. This makes both the initial backfill and any re-queue safe to re-run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the activation order: the enrichment queue listener MUST be deployed and active before the seed backfill begins, so any ticket created or updated during seeding is captured and does not fall through the gap.
findings.finding: The plan is silent on the dual-write window: tickets created or updated AFTER the backfill seed starts but BEFORE the event-sourced enrichment queue is live may never receive a digest, leaving permanent gaps in the corpus.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically'
findings.suggested_fix: In the deployment runbook (S7 ADR or a deploy note), add: 'Step 1: deploy queue listener. Step 2: verify listener is consuming events. Step 3: trigger seed backfill.' This ensures zero window between listener activation and seed start.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a rollback / cleanup path for digest sidecar data: specify digest schema versioning (e.g., a `digest_version` field), how stale digests are detected and re-generated on format change, and what the store state looks like after a full feature rollback (digests deleted vs. left inert).
findings.finding: The plan provides no rollback path for the persisted digest sidecar data: if the Cupid digest schema or format changes post-deploy, there is no described process for invalidating, migrating, or removing stale digests, and the post-rollback schema state is unspecified.
findings.location: Design section — 'digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar'; Success Criteria — ADR in S7
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `digest_schema_version` field to every Cupid digest record. The BM25F indexer and judge skip any digest whose version does not match the current expected version, treating it as absent (triggers re-enrichment). On rollback, document that existing digests are left in place as inert data (no functional impact since the consumer is removed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Python library that provides BM25F (field-weighted) retrieval and verify it is available as a pip dependency, OR explicitly scope S5 to implement a bespoke BM25F scorer and add a SPIKE proving recall@20 ≥ X on a sample of the rebar ticket corpus before committing the full S1–S7 build.
findings.finding: The plan commits to 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests' as the Stage-1 retrieval mechanism but names no Python library providing BM25F (the field-weighted variant of BM25), and no such library exists anywhere in pyproject.toml or src/rebar. Standard pure-Python BM25 libraries (rank_bm25, bm25s) provide BM25Okapi/BM25L/BM25Plus — not BM25F with true multi-field weighting. The plan asserts this as a concrete design choice across S1–S7, not as a deferred decision.
findings.location: Design section — 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank: … (1) brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `rank-bm25>=0.7` or `bm25s` to pyproject.toml's agents/overlap extra and note that you will use BM25Okapi (and adjust the field-weighting claim to match what the library actually provides), or (b) rename the mechanism to 'BM25-based' and route the field-weighting design to a spike in S5 with an explicit recall gate before S6 and S7 commit to it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the LLM pairwise judge's output relation enum with `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` (all 6 values: `blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`), and specify that `relates_to` is the correct classification for 'related-but-distinct' pairs that must NOT be surfaced as overlap suggestions.
findings.finding: The LLM pairwise judge's relation enum is under-specified relative to the live CANONICAL_RELATIONS: the plan names only three output values (`duplicates`, `supersedes`, `depends_on`) but `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` in `src/rebar/graph/_links.py` defines six values (`blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`). The omission of `relates_to` is especially load-bearing: it is the canonical value for 'related-but-distinct' work, which is the exact precision case the Success Criteria require the judge to classify correctly (SC3: 'a related-but-distinct pair must not be surfaced'). Without `relates_to` in the enum, the judge has no output value for this class and must either invent one or map it to a wrong relation, corrupting the `rebar link` command generated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §AC2 / Success Criteria §SC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the judge output schema section (to be defined in S6/S7), declare the relation enum as exactly `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` minus any domain-inapplicable values, explicitly state `relates_to` → 'do not surface' and use it as the abstain/no-overlap label, ensuring generated `rebar link` commands always use canonical values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the overlap suggestion item schema (field vocabulary: at minimum `candidate_ticket_id`, `suggested_relation`, `rationale`, `rebar_link_command`) co-located with AC2, so S6 and S7 share a single contract.
findings.finding: The schema for each item in the surfaced overlap output (the ≤3 advisory suggestions) is never defined. The plan asserts each suggestion includes a 'ready-to-run `rebar link` command' and a rationale, but no field vocabulary (e.g., `candidate_ticket_id`, `relation`, `rationale`, `rebar_link_command`) is specified anywhere in the parent plan. AC2 is the only normative statement and it describes behavior, not structure. An executor building the advisory output renderer has no contract to implement against.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §AC2 / Success Criteria §SC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the Cupid digest field schema (field names, types, required/optional) in the parent plan (or in a named section referenced from the Design block), so the digest-extraction LLM prompt and the BM25F field weights are specified against the same vocabulary.
findings.finding: The Cupid digest field schema — the LLM-extracted structured representation used as both BM25F input and the stage-1 corpus — is never defined at the parent plan level. The Design section names it 'LLM-extracted Cupid digest' and the ADR (S7) is cited as the eventual documentation target, but no field vocabulary is given. The BM25F stage requires named fields with differential weights (referenced implicitly via child-story cache entries mentioning `problem_keywords`, `component_or_area`, `propositions`, `key_entities`), yet an executor cannot know what to extract, what the LLM prompt must produce, or how the digest is structured without reading child stories.
findings.location: Design section ('each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior for each LLM sub-step failure (digest extraction failure and pairwise judge failure), at minimum stating whether the pipeline returns empty suggestions and logs a warning, or propagates an error.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for any LLM sub-step failure within the pipeline. The plan names two LLM calls — (1) digest extraction and (2) the bounded pairwise judge — but neither the Design section nor the Success/Acceptance Criteria state what happens when either call fails (timeout, rate-limit, malformed JSON output, or model refusal). The plan is advisory-only by design, which makes a 'skip gracefully' fallback natural, but that behavior is not stated and an executor cannot distinguish 'return empty suggestions on failure' from 'surface an error to the reviewer' from 'abort the store-wide step'.
findings.location: Design section (two-stage pipeline); Success Criteria §SC4 ('advisory-only, never blocking')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design or SC4: 'On any LLM sub-step failure (timeout, malformed output, rate-limit), the step emits zero overlap suggestions and logs a non-blocking warning; the claim-gate is unaffected.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
